docs.title: ROS 2 Connected Course (Humble, Jammy)
docs.text:
This ROS 2 course is designed to run on Linux, Windows (WSL2 + WSLg), and macOS (XQuartz) - all using one Docker image.

This repository contains tutorials, example code, and a Docker environment to help you learn and experiment with ROS 2 Humble Hawksbill and related tools (RViz2, Gazebo Classic, colcon, rosdep, etc.).

For more information about ROS 2 Humble, visit the official documentation: 👉 https://docs.ros.org/en/humble

docs.title: 📘 Course Overview
docs.text:
This project is part of coursework for CMPE 249 - Intelligent Autonomous Systems at San José State University (SJSU).

It provides a structured, classroom-ready introduction to ROS 2 with practical exercises in simulation, perception, and control.

  • Start with Tutorial 0 → Pull and run the Docker container.
  • Then follow Tutorials 1-13 in order for progressive learning.
  • Compatible with Linux, Windows, and macOS using containerization.

docs.title: 🐳 Docker Image Information
docs.text:
Pre-built multi-architecture image available on Docker Hub:

Repository: 

ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo:jammy

Pull command: 

docker pull ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo:jammy

The image automatically detects and runs on: - amd64 (Intel / AMD laptops & desktops) - arm64 (Apple Silicon, Jetson boards, etc.)

No authentication or keys are needed - the image is public.

docs.title: Linux (Ubuntu / Debian)
docs.text:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install ca-certificates curl gnupg
sudo install -m 0755 -d /etc/apt/keyrings
curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.gpg
echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/docker.gpg] https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu $(. /etc/os-release && echo "$VERSION_CODENAME") stable" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list > /dev/null
sudo apt update
sudo apt install docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io docker-buildx-plugin docker-compose-plugin
Then add yourself to the Docker group: 
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
newgrp docker

docs.title: Windows 11 / 10
docs.text:
1. Install Docker Desktop → https://www.docker.com/products/docker-desktop
  2. Enable WSL2 and install Ubuntu from the Microsoft Store.
  3. Open Ubuntu (WSL2) and follow the Linux quick start below.

docs.title: macOS
docs.text:
1. Install Docker Desktop for Mac → https://www.docker.com/products/docker-desktop
  2. Install XQuartz for GUI apps → https://www.xquartz.org/
  3. Restart your Mac after installation.

docs.title: 🐧 Linux (X11)
docs.text:
xhost +local:
docker run -it --rm --name ros2course   -e DISPLAY=$DISPLAY -e QT_X11_NO_MITSHM=1   -v /tmp/.X11-unix:/tmp/.X11-unix:ro   -v $(pwd):/home/ros/ros2_tutorial   --device /dev/dri   ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo:jammy

docs.title: 🪟 Windows 11 (WSL2 + WSLg)
docs.text: docker run -it --rm --name ros2course   -e DISPLAY=$DISPLAY   -e WAYLAND_DISPLAY=$WAYLAND_DISPLAY   -e XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR   -e PULSE_SERVER=$PULSE_SERVER   -v /tmp/.X11-unix:/tmp/.X11-unix   -v /mnt/wslg:/mnt/wslg   -v $(pwd):/home/ros/ros2_tutorial   --device /dev/dri --device /dev/dxg   ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo:jammy

docs.title: 🍎 macOS (XQuartz)
docs.text:
xhost + 127.0.0.1
docker run -it --rm --name ros2course   -e DISPLAY=host.docker.internal:0   -e QT_X11_NO_MITSHM=1   -v $(pwd):/home/ros/ros2_tutorial   ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo:jammy

docs.title: 🧠 Using the Container
docs.text:
Open a new terminal into the same container: 

docker exec -it ros2course bash
source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

Inside the container you can run: 

rviz2                # Open RViz2 GUI
gazebo               # Open Gazebo Classic
ros2 run demo_nodes_cpp talker
ros2 run demo_nodes_cpp listener

docs.title: 🔍 Verify GUI
docs.text:
xeyes        # tiny X11 test window
glxinfo -B   # check OpenGL renderer info

docs.title: 📚 Tutorials
docs.text:
All tutorials are in the docs/ folder and published online:

👉 https://ambarishgk007.github.io/ROS2-Tutorial/

Tutorial Topic 0 Getting Started 1 Nodes, Topics, and Services 2 Parameters and Launch Files 3 Create a Package (Pub/Sub) 4 Services and Actions 5 TF2 and RViz2 6 URDF and Robot Description 7 Gazebo - Empty World & Spawn 8 Gazebo Sensors and Plugins 9 Navigation (Nav2) 10 Record & Replay (rosbag2) 11 RQT, RViz, and TF Debugging 12 Custom Interfaces and Lifecycle 13 Multi-Robot and Networking

docs.title: 🧩 Support & Contribution
docs.text:
• To fix typos or contribute new tutorials, open a Pull Request.
  • To rebuild the Docker image locally: 
    docker build -t ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo:jammy -f docker/Dockerfile .

docs.title: 🏁 Summary
docs.text:
✔️ Prebuilt ROS 2 Humble environment with GUI tools ✔️ Works across Linux, macOS, and Windows ✔️ Includes all core tutorials 0-13 ✔️ Open-source and free to use for learning

Enjoy your journey through ROS 2 Connected Course!

docs.title: 🎓 Course Information & Acknowledgment
docs.text:
This project was developed as part of the course CMPE 249 - Intelligent Autonomous Systems Department of Computer Engineering, San José State University (SJSU)

Instructor: Dr. Kaikai Liu - Course Instructor, CMPE 249 (Guided the course framework, robotics lab setup, and simulation-based learning environment.)

Student / Developer: Ambarish G.K - Developer of the ROS 2 Connected Course environment, Docker setup, and documentation.

Term: Fall 2025 Institution: San José State University, California, USA

Author: Ambarish G.K LinkedIn: Ambarish G.K Docker Hub: ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo

docs.title: Tutorial 0: Getting Started - Pull, Run, and Use the Course Container
docs.text:
This course uses a prebuilt Docker image so everyone has the same working environment on Linux, Windows (WSL2 + WSLg), and macOS (XQuartz).

  • Image: ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo:jammy (multi‑arch: amd64 + arm64)
  • Container name (we’ll reuse it): ros2course

Environment Note - Make sure the course container is running (see Tutorial 0). - Open a new terminal in the same container with: docker exec -it ros2course bash - In each new terminal: source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

docs.title: Pull the image (all OS)
docs.text: docker pull ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo:jammy

docs.title: Linux (X11)
docs.text:
xhost +local:
docker run -it --rm   --name ros2course   -e DISPLAY=$DISPLAY -e QT_X11_NO_MITSHM=1   -v /tmp/.X11-unix:/tmp/.X11-unix:ro   -v $(pwd):/home/ros/ros2_tutorial   --device /dev/dri   ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo:jammy

docs.title: Windows 11 (WSL2 + WSLg) - run inside your WSL Ubuntu
docs.text: docker run -it --rm   --name ros2course   -e DISPLAY=$DISPLAY   -e WAYLAND_DISPLAY=$WAYLAND_DISPLAY   -e XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR   -e PULSE_SERVER=$PULSE_SERVER   -v /tmp/.X11-unix:/tmp/.X11-unix   -v /mnt/wslg:/mnt/wslg   -v $(pwd):/home/ros/ros2_tutorial   --device /dev/dri --device /dev/dxg   ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo:jammy

docs.title: macOS (with XQuartz)
docs.text:
1) Install + start XQuartz, enable Allow connections from network clients. 2) In Terminal: 

xhost + 127.0.0.1
docker run -it --rm   --name ros2course   -e DISPLAY=host.docker.internal:0   -e QT_X11_NO_MITSHM=1   -v $(pwd):/home/ros/ros2_tutorial   ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo:jammy

docs.title: New terminal for the same container
docs.text:
On the host: 

docker exec -it ros2course bash

docs.title: Sanity checks inside the container
docs.text:
source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash
rviz2                # GUI should open
ros2 --help

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Containers eliminate 'works on my machine' issues and give you identical ROS 2 + GUI tools anywhere.

docs.title: How you’ll use it
docs.text: You’ll do all tutorials inside this container. Use docker exec whenever you need another terminal.

docs.title: Wrap‑up
docs.text:
• You can pull and run the course image on any OS.
  • You know how to attach more terminals to the same container.
  • You verified GUI support (RViz).

Next: Go to Tutorial 1 - Nodes, Topics, and Services

docs.title: Tutorial 10: Record and Replay with rosbag2
docs.text: Environment Note - Make sure the course container is running (see Tutorial 0). - Open a new terminal in the same container with: docker exec -it ros2course bash - In each new terminal: source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

docs.title: Concept Overview
docs.text: rosbag2 records topics to disk for debugging, datasets, and reproducibility.

docs.title: Record
docs.text: ros2 bag record -o my_bag /tf /tf_static /scan /cmd_vel /odom

docs.title: Replay
docs.text: ros2 bag play my_bag

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Time‑shifting lets you debug without rerunning the robot.

docs.title: How you’ll use it
docs.text: You’ll capture scenarios and replay them to test your code deterministically.

docs.title: Wrap‑up
docs.text:
• You recorded multiple topics to a bag.
  • You replayed data to reproduce scenarios.
  • You can now iterate faster on algorithms.

Next: Tutorial 11 - RQT, RViz, TF Debugging

docs.title: Tutorial 11: RQT, RViz, and TF Debugging
docs.text: Environment Note - Make sure the course container is running (see Tutorial 0). - Open a new terminal in the same container with: docker exec -it ros2course bash - In each new terminal: source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

docs.title: Concept Overview
docs.text: Use RQT GUIs and RViz to diagnose graphs, plots, logs, and transforms quickly.

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
rqt_graph
rqt_plot
ros2 run tf2_tools view_frames
rviz2

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• rqt_graph shows node–topic connections.
  • rqt_plot plots topic values over time.
  • view_frames generates a TF tree pdf.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Visual tools reduce guesswork and expose misconfigurations.

docs.title: How you’ll use it
docs.text: You’ll use these constantly when integrating teams’ nodes and params.

docs.title: Wrap‑up
docs.text:
• You can inspect connections and signals.
  • You can visualize TF trees and displays.
  • You can spot mismatched topics quickly.

Next: Tutorial 12 - Custom Interfaces & Lifecycle

docs.title: Tutorial 12: Custom Interfaces and Lifecycle Nodes
docs.text: Environment Note - Make sure the course container is running (see Tutorial 0). - Open a new terminal in the same container with: docker exec -it ros2course bash - In each new terminal: source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

docs.title: Concept Overview
docs.text: Custom messages/services define your robot’s APIs. Lifecycle nodes formalize startup/teardown states (configure → activate → deactivate).

docs.title: Custom message
docs.text:
Create package my_interfaces, add msg/Num.msg: 

int64 num
Build and use it in a pub/sub node.

docs.title: Lifecycle flow
docs.text:
ros2 run lifecycle demos_lifecycle_talker
ros2 lifecycle list /talker
ros2 lifecycle set /talker configure
ros2 lifecycle set /talker activate

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Clean, explicit interfaces make systems reusable; lifecycle makes startup predictable.

docs.title: How you’ll use it
docs.text: Define messages once, reuse everywhere; gate publishers/subscribers with lifecycle state.

docs.title: Wrap‑up
docs.text:
• You created a custom interface.
  • You practiced lifecycle transitions.
  • You prepared nodes for real deployments.

Next: Tutorial 13 - Multi‑Robot & Networking

docs.title: Tutorial 13: Multi‑Robot and Networking
docs.text: Environment Note - Make sure the course container is running (see Tutorial 0). - Open a new terminal in the same container with: docker exec -it ros2course bash - In each new terminal: source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

docs.title: Concept Overview
docs.text: Multi‑robot work needs unique namespaces, discovery settings, and sometimes separate domains.

docs.title: Namespaces
docs.text:
ros2 run gazebo_ros spawn_entity.py -file /home/ros/ros2_tutorial/examples/simple_robot.urdf -entity robot1 --ros-args -r __ns:=/robot1
ros2 run gazebo_ros spawn_entity.py -file /home/ros/ros2_tutorial/examples/simple_robot.urdf -entity robot2 --ros-args -r __ns:=/robot2

docs.title: Domains
docs.text: export ROS_DOMAIN_ID=7

docs.title: Test
docs.text:
ros2 topic echo /robot1/scan
ros2 topic echo /robot2/scan

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Avoid topic collisions and control who talks to whom across networks.

docs.title: How you’ll use it
docs.text: Use namespaces and domains to isolate or connect robots in labs and competitions.

docs.title: Wrap‑up
docs.text:
• You ran multiple robots with namespaces.
  • You controlled discovery with domains.
  • You validated cross‑robot topics.

Next: You’ve completed the course!

docs.title: Tutorial 1: Nodes, Topics, and Services (with Turtlesim)
docs.text: Environment Note - Make sure the course container is running (see Tutorial 0). - Open a new terminal in the same container with: docker exec -it ros2course bash - In each new terminal: source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

docs.title: Concept Overview
docs.text: A node is a running process. Nodes communicate using topics (pub/sub) and services (request/response). This separation lets you build modular robots where sensors, control, and UI are independent.

docs.title: Run a node (turtlesim)
docs.text:
ros2 run turtlesim turtlesim_node
Open a new terminal and list nodes: 
ros2 node list

docs.title: Teleop node
docs.text:
ros2 run turtlesim turtle_teleop_key
Move the turtle with arrow keys.

docs.title: Command Breakdown
docs.text:
• ros2 run PACKAGE EXECUTABLE starts a node.
  • ros2 node list shows running nodes.
  • Two nodes are now running and communicating indirectly.

docs.title: Observe topics & services
docs.text:
ros2 topic list
ros2 topic echo /turtle1/pose
ros2 service list
ros2 service call /spawn turtlesim/srv/Spawn "{x: 2, y: 2, theta: 0.2, name: 't2'}"

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Topics decouple producers and consumers; services handle point-in-time operations (like spawning objects).

docs.title: How you’ll use it
docs.text: Nearly every sensor publishes on a topic (e.g., /scan). Your code will subscribe to those or provide services.

docs.title: Wrap‑up
docs.text:
• You ran two nodes and saw them interact.
  • You inspected topics and called a service.
  • You understand node boundaries.

Next: Tutorial 2 - Parameters and Launch Files

docs.title: Tutorial 2: Parameters and Launch Files
docs.text: Environment Note - Make sure the course container is running (see Tutorial 0). - Open a new terminal in the same container with: docker exec -it ros2course bash - In each new terminal: source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

docs.title: Concept Overview
docs.text: Parameters are node settings you can change without recompiling. Launch files start many nodes with configs in one command.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
ros2 param list
ros2 param set /turtlesim background_r 255
ros2 param set /turtlesim background_g 100

docs.title: Launch multiple nodes
docs.text:
Use the provided example: 

ros2 launch /home/ros/ros2_tutorial/examples/turtlesim_start.launch.py

docs.title: Command Breakdown
docs.text:
• ros2 param lets you inspect/change runtime settings.
  • ros2 launch runs multi-node systems with parameters and remappings.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Real robots run many nodes; launch files give you a reproducible recipe.

docs.title: How you’ll use it
docs.text: You’ll bundle your systems into launch files to start everything consistently on any machine.

docs.title: Wrap‑up
docs.text:
• You tuned parameters live.
  • You launched multiple nodes at once.
  • You learned how to scale beyond one terminal per node.

Next: Tutorial 3 - Create a Package and Pub/Sub

docs.title: Tutorial 3: Create a Package and Pub/Sub Nodes (Python)
docs.text: Environment Note - Make sure the course container is running (see Tutorial 0). - Open a new terminal in the same container with: docker exec -it ros2course bash - In each new terminal: source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

docs.title: Concept Overview
docs.text: Packages group code, configs, and launch files. The publisher–subscriber pattern is the core of ROS 2 messaging.

docs.title: Create and build
docs.text:
mkdir -p ~/ros2_ws/src
cp -r /home/ros/ros2_tutorial/examples/my_first_pkg ~/ros2_ws/src/
cd ~/ros2_ws
colcon build
source install/setup.bash

docs.title: Run them
docs.text:
Terminal A: 

ros2 run my_first_pkg publisher_member_function
Terminal B: 
ros2 run my_first_pkg subscriber_member_function

docs.title: Code walkthrough
docs.text:
• create_publisher(String, 'chatter', 10): topic type, name, queue.
  • create_timer(0.5, ...): publish every 0.5s.
  • create_subscription(..., 'chatter', ...): receives messages and logs them.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Pub/sub lets you connect many producers and consumers without tight coupling.

docs.title: How you’ll use it
docs.text: Most of your stack (sensors, planners, controllers) communicates with pub/sub.

docs.title: Wrap‑up
docs.text:
• You built a package and ran custom nodes.
  • You understood basic rclpy APIs.
  • You saw messages flow over a topic.

Next: Tutorial 4 - Services and Actions

docs.title: Tutorial 4: Services and Actions
docs.text: Environment Note - Make sure the course container is running (see Tutorial 0). - Open a new terminal in the same container with: docker exec -it ros2course bash - In each new terminal: source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

docs.title: Concept Overview
docs.text: Services are synchronous request/response (like a function call). Actions are for long‑running goals with feedback (like navigation).

docs.title: Service example
docs.text:
Terminal A: 

python3 /home/ros/ros2_tutorial/examples/add_two_ints_server.py
Terminal B: 
python3 /home/ros/ros2_tutorial/examples/add_two_ints_client.py

docs.title: Code walkthrough
docs.text:
• Server: create_service(AddTwoInts, 'add_two_ints', callback)
  • Client: waits for service, sends request, prints result.

docs.title: Actions (explore)
docs.text:
ros2 action list
ros2 action info /turtle1/rotate_absolute

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Some tasks need a clear request/response or progress feedback (e.g., move to pose).

docs.title: How you’ll use it
docs.text: Use services to configure or trigger events; use actions for goals that take time.

docs.title: Wrap‑up
docs.text:
• You implemented a service server and client.
  • You learned when to use actions vs services.
  • You explored turtlesim’s action interface.

Next: Tutorial 5 - TF2 and RViz2

docs.title: Tutorial 5: TF2 and RViz2 Visualization
docs.text: Environment Note - Make sure the course container is running (see Tutorial 0). - Open a new terminal in the same container with: docker exec -it ros2course bash - In each new terminal: source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

docs.title: Concept Overview
docs.text: TF2 tracks coordinate frames so nodes agree on where things are. RViz2 lets you visualize data and frames.

docs.title: Launch demo
docs.text:
ros2 launch /home/ros/ros2_tutorial/examples/tf_demo.launch.py
Then open RViz2 and add the TF display.

docs.title: Inspect transforms
docs.text: ros2 run tf2_ros tf2_echo base_link camera_link

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Navigation, manipulation, and perception all depend on consistent frames.

docs.title: How you’ll use it
docs.text: You’ll broadcast static and dynamic transforms so all nodes share a common spatial view.

docs.title: Wrap‑up
docs.text:
• You launched a TF demo and viewed frames.
  • You inspected a transform on the CLI.
  • You used RViz2 for visualization.

Next: Tutorial 6 - URDF and Robot Description

docs.title: Tutorial 6: URDF and Robot Description
docs.text: Environment Note - Make sure the course container is running (see Tutorial 0). - Open a new terminal in the same container with: docker exec -it ros2course bash - In each new terminal: source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

docs.title: Concept Overview
docs.text: URDF (XML) describes your robot’s links and joints. This is the model that RViz/Gazebo and many tools consume.

docs.title: Inspect the example
docs.text:
Open: 

/home/ros/ros2_tutorial/examples/simple_robot.urdf

docs.title: Visualize in RViz2
docs.text: ros2 launch urdf_tutorial display.launch.py model:=/home/ros/ros2_tutorial/examples/simple_robot.urdf

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: A robot description is the single source of truth for geometry and kinematics.

docs.title: How you’ll use it
docs.text: You’ll add sensors/links here and reuse it in simulation and real robots.

docs.title: Wrap‑up
docs.text:
• You learned what URDF is and opened a sample model.
  • You viewed the model in RViz2.
  • You’re ready to take it into a simulator.

Next: Tutorial 7 - Gazebo: Empty World & Spawn

docs.title: Tutorial 7: Gazebo - Empty World & Spawn
docs.text: Environment Note - Make sure the course container is running (see Tutorial 0). - Open a new terminal in the same container with: docker exec -it ros2course bash - In each new terminal: source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

docs.title: Concept Overview
docs.text: Gazebo (classic) simulates physics and sensors so you can test without hardware.

docs.title: Start an empty world
docs.text: ros2 launch /home/ros/ros2_tutorial/examples/empty_world.launch.py

docs.title: Spawn your URDF
docs.text: ros2 run gazebo_ros spawn_entity.py   -file /home/ros/ros2_tutorial/examples/simple_robot.urdf   -entity simple_bot

docs.title: Verify topics
docs.text: ros2 topic list

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Simulation lets you iterate quickly and safely.

docs.title: How you’ll use it
docs.text: You’ll spawn and respawn robots while developing perception and control.

docs.title: Wrap‑up
docs.text:
• You ran Gazebo and spawned your robot.
  • You saw simulator topics appear in ROS 2.
  • You can change spawn poses and respawn.

Next: Tutorial 8 - Gazebo: Sensors and Plugins

docs.title: Tutorial 8: Gazebo - Sensors and Plugins
docs.text: Environment Note - Make sure the course container is running (see Tutorial 0). - Open a new terminal in the same container with: docker exec -it ros2course bash - In each new terminal: source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

docs.title: Concept Overview
docs.text: Gazebo plugins attach simulated sensors/actuators to links. They produce ROS topics just like the real hardware drivers.

docs.title: Add a LiDAR
docs.text:
See: 

/home/ros/ros2_tutorial/examples/simple_robot_with_sensors.urdf
Spawn: 
ros2 run gazebo_ros spawn_entity.py   -file /home/ros/ros2_tutorial/examples/simple_robot_with_sensors.urdf   -entity sensor_bot

docs.title: Watch the data
docs.text:
ros2 topic list | grep scan
ros2 topic echo /scan

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Sensors are the inputs to autonomy; you’ll test algorithms before touching real hardware.

docs.title: How you’ll use it
docs.text: You’ll publish scans/images/IMU data from Gazebo and consume them in your nodes.

docs.title: Wrap‑up
docs.text:
• You added a LiDAR plugin and viewed /scan.
  • You understand plugin-produced topics.
  • You can extend the URDF with more sensors.

Next: Tutorial 9 - Navigation (Nav2)

docs.title: Tutorial 9: Navigation (Nav2) in Simulation
docs.text: Environment Note - Make sure the course container is running (see Tutorial 0). - Open a new terminal in the same container with: docker exec -it ros2course bash - In each new terminal: source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

docs.title: Concept Overview
docs.text: Nav2 moves a robot to a goal using mapping, planning, and control. It consumes sensor topics and TF.

docs.title: Launch Nav2 (with sim time)
docs.text:
ros2 launch nav2_bringup bringup_launch.py use_sim_time:=True
Send a goal: 
ros2 action send_goal /navigate_to_pose nav2_msgs/action/NavigateToPose "{pose: {pose: {position: {x: 1.0, y: 0.5, z: 0.0}, orientation: {w: 1.0}}}}"

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Autonomy relies on accurate transforms and sensor data to plan safe paths.

docs.title: How you’ll use it
docs.text: You’ll integrate your URDF + Gazebo sensors with Nav2 for closed-loop navigation.

docs.title: Wrap‑up
docs.text:
• You started Nav2 and sent a goal.
  • You saw how actions control long-running tasks.
  • You integrated sim time with the stack.

Next: Tutorial 10 - rosbag2

docs.title: 🧭 ROS 2 + Docker Cheatsheet (Humble / Jammy)
docs.text: A quick reference for running, managing, and testing your ROS 2 environment inside the ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo:jammy Docker image.

docs.title: 🔹 Pull & Run the Container
docs.text:
# Pull the prebuilt image (multi-arch: amd64 + arm64)
docker pull ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo:jammy

# Run interactively (Linux example)
xhost +local:
docker run -it --rm --name ros2course   -e DISPLAY=$DISPLAY -e QT_X11_NO_MITSHM=1   -v /tmp/.X11-unix:/tmp/.X11-unix:ro   -v $(pwd):/home/ros/ros2_tutorial   --device /dev/dri   ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo:jammy

docs.title: 🔹 Container Management
docs.text:
docker ps                    # list running containers
docker exec -it ros2course bash   # open a new terminal inside the same container
docker stop ros2course       # stop container manually
docker rm ros2course         # remove stopped container
docker images                # list downloaded images
docker rmi <image_id>        # remove an image

docs.title: 🔹 Build Your Own (optional)
docs.text: docker build -t my-ros2-image:jammy -f docker/Dockerfile .

docs.title: 🔹 Environment Setup
docs.text:
source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

✅ Tip: In this image, the ROS 2 environment is already auto-sourced in .bashrc.

docs.title: 🔹 Quick Sanity Tests
docs.text:
rviz2               # Launch RViz2 GUI
gazebo              # Launch Gazebo Classic
xeyes               # (tiny X11 test window)
glxinfo -B          # Check OpenGL renderer info

docs.title: 🔹 Nodes, Topics, Services
docs.text:
ros2 node list
ros2 topic list
ros2 service list
ros2 action list

docs.title: 🔹 Inspecting Data
docs.text:
ros2 topic echo /topic_name
ros2 topic info /topic_name
ros2 interface show geometry_msgs/msg/Twist

docs.title: 🔹 Launching Demos
docs.text:
ros2 run demo_nodes_cpp talker
ros2 run demo_nodes_cpp listener
ros2 launch demo_nodes_cpp talk_listen.launch.py

docs.title: 🔹 Create a Workspace
docs.text:
mkdir -p ~/ros2_ws/src
cd ~/ros2_ws/src
ros2 pkg create --build-type ament_python my_pkg

docs.title: 🔹 Build & Source
docs.text:
cd ~/ros2_ws
colcon build
source install/setup.bash

docs.title: 🔹 Parameters
docs.text:
ros2 param list
ros2 param get /node_name param_name
ros2 param set /node_name param_name value

docs.title: 🔹 rosbag2 Recording
docs.text:
ros2 bag record -a                      # record all topics
ros2 bag record /topic1 /topic2         # record selected topics
ros2 bag info <bag_name>
ros2 bag play <bag_name>

docs.title: 🔹 TF2 Utilities
docs.text:
ros2 run tf2_tools view_frames          # generate TF tree PDF
ros2 run tf2_ros tf2_echo base_link camera_link

docs.title: 🧰 Useful Shortcuts
docs.text: Task Command Open new terminal in same container docker exec -it ros2course bash Save container state as image docker commit ros2course my-snapshot:latest Clean all stopped containers docker container prune Clean dangling images docker image prune

docs.title: ⚡ Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Problem Solution GUI apps not opening (Linux) Run xhost +local: before docker run GUI apps not opening (Windows WSL2) Run container inside WSL; check /mnt/wslg exists macOS blank window Ensure XQuartz is running (xhost + 127.0.0.1) Permission denied (Docker) Add user to Docker group → sudo usermod -aG docker $USER

Author: Ambarish G.K Docker Hub: ambarishgk007/ros2-humble-rviz-gazebo

docs.title: YOLO Example (ROS 2 + Docker)
docs.text:
This example shows how to run a pre-baked YOLOv8 demo inside a Docker image, view annotated video in RViz, and understand the node structure so you can tweak it for your projects. No Docker build steps here—just pull and run the published image.

Image used: docker pull ambarishgk007/ros2-yolo:humble

docs.title: What you’ll run
docs.text:
• A ROS 2 node video_yolo that:
  • Reads a video (.mp4) with OpenCV.
  • Runs YOLOv8 object detection (Ultralytics).
  • Publishes annotated frames to /image_yolo (sensor_msgs/Image).
  • Publishes detections (class + confidence + bbox) to:
    • /detections_yolo (vision_msgs/Detection2DArray) if available or
    • /detections_yolo_json (std_msgs/String) as a JSON fallback.
  • RViz opens with a pre-configured view that subscribes to /image_yolo.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Linux desktop with Docker and X11.
  • Allow X11 access (once per session): 
    xhost +local:root

docs.title: Quickstart (pull & run)
docs.text:
Pull the prebaked image and run the example (no mounts required):

docker pull ambarishgk007/ros2-yolo:humble

docker run --rm -it   --env DISPLAY=$DISPLAY   --env QT_X11_NO_MITSHM=1   -v /tmp/.X11-unix:/tmp/.X11-unix:rw   --name ros2-yolo   ambarishgk007/ros2-yolo:humble

What happens

  • Launch file starts: ros2 launch image_demo yolo_demo.launch.py
  • The node reads /ws/src/ROS2-Tutorial/examples/data/sample.mp4 inside the image.
  • Detections are rendered on frames → /image_yolo.
  • RViz opens and displays /image_yolo automatically.

If you see OpenGL warnings (e.g., “failed to load driver: iris”), the container will fall back to software rendering. You can ignore these or add --device /dev/dri:/dev/dri to try hardware GL on Intel/AMD.

docs.title: Use your own data (mount + override)
docs.text:
Mount a folder from your host and pass paths via launch args:

docker run --rm -it   --env DISPLAY=$DISPLAY   -v /tmp/.X11-unix:/tmp/.X11-unix:rw   -v $(pwd)/examples/data:/data   --name ros2-yolo   ambarishgk007/ros2-yolo:humble   ros2 launch image_demo yolo_demo.launch.py     video_path:=/data/sample.mp4     model_path:=/data/yolov8n.pt     conf_thres:=0.35
  • video_path: any OpenCV-readable video (.mp4, etc.).
  • model_path: YOLOv8 model weights (e.g., yolov8n.pt).
  • conf_thres: detection confidence threshold (0–1).

docs.title: Topics
docs.text:
Topic Type Description /image_yolo sensor_msgs/Image Annotated frames with bounding boxes /detections_yolo vision_msgs/Detection2DArray Structured detections (if vision_msgs present) /detections_yolo_json std_msgs/String JSON list of {label, conf, bbox_xyxy}

List topics / inspect messages: 

ros2 topic list
ros2 topic echo /detections_yolo

docs.title: video_yolo node (Python, rclpy)
docs.text:
Responsibilities 1. Capture frames with OpenCV (cv2.VideoCapture). 2. Run YOLOv8 inference (Ultralytics YOLO(model_path); predict(conf=...)). 3. Draw boxes + labels on a copy of the frame. 4. Publish annotated sensor_msgs/Image via cv_bridge. 5. Publish detections: - Preferred: vision_msgs/Detection2DArray (class + score + bbox). - Fallback: JSON list on std_msgs/String if vision_msgs is not installed.

Parameters (declared & read via ROS 2 params) - video_path (string): absolute path to the input video. - model_path (string): path to .pt weights. - conf_thres (float): confidence threshold (default 0.25).

QoS / Frequency - Publishes at the video’s FPS (falls back to ~30 FPS if metadata is odd). - QoS depth = 10 on image & detection pubs (good default for RViz).

The node tries to loop the video when it reaches the end.

docs.title: RViz configuration
docs.text:
The launch file opens RViz with a minimal config that subscribes to /image_yolo. If you prefer manual control: 

rviz2
Then: Add → Image → Image Topic = /image_yolo.

docs.title: How the image is structured (for understanding/tweaks)
docs.text:
You don’t need this to run the example—but it helps when customizing.

  • Base: osrf/ros:humble-desktop (Ubuntu 22.04 + RViz + ROS 2 tools).
  • System ROS packages: cv_bridge, vision_msgs, image_transport.
  • Python deps live in a virtualenv at /opt/venv (to avoid apt/pip conflicts):
  • opencv-python, ultralytics (with torch, torchvision).
  • The image sets: 
    PATH=/opt/venv/bin:$PATH
PYTHONPATH=/opt/venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages:$PYTHONPATH
    so ament/ROS entry points can import Ultralytics.
  • Workspace layout inside the container:
  • /ws/src/ROS2-Tutorial/ → your repo copy (including image_demo, ros2demo, examples/data).
  • Built with colcon build --symlink-install.
  • Entrypoint:
  • Sources /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash and /ws/install/setup.bash.
  • Default CMD (what runs when you docker run the image): 
    ros2 launch image_demo yolo_demo.launch.py     video_path:=/ws/src/ROS2-Tutorial/examples/data/sample.mp4     model_path:=/ws/src/ROS2-Tutorial/examples/data/yolov8n.pt     conf_thres:=0.25

docs.title: Customizing for your needs
docs.text:
• Change topics: edit the publishers in video_yolo.py (e.g., publish to /camera/image to integrate with other stacks).
  • Different model: mount or bake your own .pt and pass as model_path.
  • Filter classes: post-filter detections by label or cls_id before drawing/publishing.
  • Publish raw frames too: add another publisher on /image_raw (no annotations).
  • Switch to live camera: replace cv2.VideoCapture(video_path) with a device index (0) or GStreamer pipeline.
  • Structured detections: prefer vision_msgs/Detection2DArray for downstream consumers like tracking/fusion.
  • RViz layout: copy the RViz config (under image_demo/rviz) and add more panels/views.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• RViz shows warnings about OpenGL: Often harmless (software rendering). Try --device /dev/dri:/dev/dri if available.
  • No video output: check you mounted data and passed correct video_path.
  • Slow on CPU: try --gpus all (if you have CUDA support on the host) or use a smaller model (e.g., yolov8n.pt).
  • Can’t see detections: increase conf_thres downwards (e.g., 0.2) or check topics with ros2 topic list.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Add a tracker node (e.g., SORT/DeepSORT) subscribing to /detections_yolo.
  • Publish segmentation masks with a segmentation model.
  • Replace video source with a ROS camera driver (e.g., ZED/USB cam) and run detections live.

Happy hacking! 🚀
